summary:
The South Sea Bubble (1720 AD) was Britain's first great financial disaster, a speculative frenzy in shares of the South Sea Company that saw stock prices rise tenfold before collapsing catastrophically, wiping out fortunes across British society and triggering a political scandal that implicated members of Parliament and the royal court. The South Sea Company, founded in 1711 and granted a monopoly on trade with Spanish South America (which was largely illusory — the asiento treaty provided only limited slave-trading rights), offered to take over a large portion of the national debt in exchange for the right to issue new stock. When the company promoted its shares aggressively in 1720, speculative mania drove prices from £128 to £1,050 within months, before the inevitable crash left thousands ruined.

The South Sea Bubble's political fallout was severe: Chancellor of the Exchequer John Aislabie was expelled from Parliament, several ministers faced criminal charges, and the crisis brought Robert Walpole to power as Britain's first effective prime minister, establishing conventions of cabinet government that would prove enduring. Parliament passed the Bubble Act of 1720, restricting the formation of joint-stock companies without a royal charter — inadvertently hampering legitimate business formation for over a century. The South Sea Bubble, alongside the simultaneous [[event:2467|Mississippi Bubble]] in France, demonstrated the systemic dangers of speculative finance and provided early evidence that markets could fail catastrophically — lessons that would need repeated relearning.
